dialogue: A small survivor camp has formed in the parking lot of an abandoned hardware store.
People move with purpose despite their exhaustion. Water is being boiled over a fire. Supplies are being inventoried. Children are being kept occupied with simple chores.
One name keeps coming up as you walk through the camp.
'The doctor.'
'Talk to the doctor.'
'The doctor will know what to do.'
Whoever this person is, the camp seems to place an enormous amount of trust in him.

choices.text: Observe the camp before getting involved
choices.lockedText: You can't get a good read on the camp.

dialogue: You spend some time watching the camp from a distance.
The doctor is constantly moving between people.
Treating cuts. Checking fevers. Giving advice.
Yet something feels strange.
You never actually see him perform anything particularly advanced.
Most of what he's doing looks like basic first aid.
You spend time speaking with different members of the camp.
An older woman insists the doctor saved her life after a wound became infected.
'Without him I'd be dead.'
A father says the doctor helped bring his son's fever down.
But a younger survivor seems less convinced.
'Maybe he's legit,' he says quietly.
'But I've never seen him do anything a first aid manual couldn't teach.'
The stories become less consistent the more people you ask.
Some claim he worked in a hospital.
Others insist he was a surgeon.
One survivor swears he used to teach medicine.
Nobody seems to know for certain.
The doctor himself appears to have never volunteered many details.
Most people speak highly of him.
'He stopped my infection from spreading.'
'He knew exactly what medicine to use.'
'He kept my daughter alive through a fever.'
Yet every story seems to involve simple treatments.
Bandages. Cleaning wounds. Hydration.
Important work, certainly, but nothing requiring years of medical training.
You find the doctor sitting beside a teenager with a badly sprained ankle.
He finishes wrapping the injury before acknowledging you.
'Sorry,' he says. 'Been a busy day.'
He looks exhausted.
There are dark circles under his eyes and dried blood on his sleeves.
'What can I do for you?'

choices.text: Watch him work for a while

dialogue: You quietly observe him throughout the afternoon.
He's calm under pressure.
Compassionate.
Patient.
But several times he consults handwritten notes before making decisions.
At one point he hesitates when asked about medication dosages.
'County hospital,' he answers immediately.
'Worked there for years.'
The response comes quickly.
Almost too quickly.

choices.lockedText: You aren't sure what questions to ask.
You can't tell if he's nervous.

dialogue: As the conversation continues, small details begin to change.
The department he worked in changes.
The years don't quite add up.
Eventually he notices your expression.
His shoulders tense.
'Keep your voice down,' he says quietly.
The doctor waits until nobody else is nearby.
For a long moment neither of you speak.
Finally he exhales.
'I'm not a doctor.'
The words come out barely above a whisper.
'I was a receptionist.'
'A medical receptionist.'
'I answered phones. Scheduled appointments. Filed paperwork.'
'When everything collapsed, people saw the scrubs and assumed.'
'The first time I corrected them.'
'The second time too.'
'Then a little girl showed up with a fever.'
'Nobody knew what to do.'
'Nobody wanted to make a decision.'
'So I did.'
He stares across the camp.
'And after that, people kept asking.'

choices.text: Ask why he continues the lie

dialogue: 'Because people need someone to believe in.'
'Look around.'
'They've lost homes. Families. Friends.'
'Half of them are terrified every minute they're awake.'
He pauses.
'If I tell them the truth, maybe they'll forgive me.'
'Or maybe they'll panic.'
'Maybe somebody dies because nobody trusts anyone anymore.'
The secret remains between the two of you.
'I don't know how much longer I can keep this up,' he admits.
'Eventually somebody will need a real doctor.'
The camp carries on around you.
Children laugh near the fire.
Survivors prepare dinner.
Nobody knows the truth.

choices.text: Tell him to keep helping people
Tell him he should confess someday

dialogue: The revelation spreads through camp quickly.
Shock turns into arguments.
'You lied to us!'
'He still saved lives!'
'What if somebody dies because of him?'
'What if somebody already would have died without him?'
The camp fractures into heated debate.

choices.text: Leave the camp to decide his fate

dialogue: 'Maybe that's enough,' you tell him.
'You've helped people survive.'
For the first time all day, he smiles.
Tomorrow he'll continue pretending to be something he isn't.
But tomorrow he'll also continue helping people.
'You can't carry this forever,' you tell him.
He nods slowly.
'I know.'
'Maybe when things are stable.'
'Maybe then I'll tell them.'
Whether that day ever comes is another question.
You remind the camp that regardless of his title, he helped people.
The arguments gradually settle.
Trust has been damaged, but not destroyed.
The former receptionist remains, no longer a doctor, but still someone willing to help.
You leave before the argument reaches a conclusion.
As you walk away, raised voices continue behind you.
Sometimes the truth helps people.
Sometimes it only gives them something new to fight about.
You spend the day helping distribute supplies and care for survivors.
Whether the doctor is genuine or not, people are alive because someone stepped forward.
For now, that seems to be enough.
You leave the camp behind.
The doctor continues his work as you disappear down the road.
Some questions remain unanswered.
